event: Early modern humans migrate through the Arabian Peninsula, leaving stone tools at sites linked to the 'Out of Africa' dispersal.
Neolithic communities herd cattle and create rock art across the once wetter, greener Arabian interior.
The Ubaid culture from Mesopotamia establishes trading contacts along the eastern Arabian coast near the Persian Gulf.
Dilmun-linked settlements in the eastern Arabian region trade copper, dates, and pearls with Sumer and the Indus Valley.
The domestication of the camel revolutionizes desert travel and incense trade across the Arabian Peninsula.
The kingdom of Dadan flourishes at al-Ula as a key oasis stop on the frankincense trade route.
The Lihyanite kingdom controls northwestern Arabia and the lucrative caravan trade in incense and spices.
The Nabataeans expand their influence into northwest Arabia, building the rock-cut city of Hegra (Mada'in Salih).
Rome annexes the Nabataean realm, including Hegra, into the new province of Arabia Petraea.
The Christian kingdom of Aksum invades and occupies parts of the Arabian Peninsula amid regional religious conflict.
The Prophet Muhammad is born in Mecca, then a major caravan and pilgrimage center in the Hejaz.
Muhammad begins receiving the Quranic revelations near Mecca, marking the start of Islam.
The Hijra, Muhammad's migration from Mecca to Medina, establishes the first Muslim community and the Islamic calendar's start.
Muslim forces under Muhammad take control of Mecca, and the Kaaba becomes the central shrine of Islam.
Muhammad dies in Medina, and the Rashidun Caliphate begins uniting the Arabian Peninsula under Islam.
The capital of the Islamic state shifts to Damascus under the Umayyads, reducing Arabia's political centrality.
Under the Abbasids the political center moves toward Baghdad, while Mecca and Medina remain the spiritual heart of Islam.
The Qarmatians sack Mecca and carry off the Black Stone from the Kaaba for over two decades.
The Rasulids and later Egyptian-based Mamluks assert influence over the Hejaz and the holy cities.
The Ottoman Empire absorbs the Hejaz and assumes guardianship of Mecca and Medina after conquering Mamluk Egypt.
Muhammad ibn Saud becomes ruler of the small oasis town of Diriyah in the Najd region.
Muhammad ibn Saud allies with reformer Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, founding the First Saudi State.
Saudi-Wahhabi forces capture Mecca, extending the First Saudi State across much of Arabia.
Ottoman-Egyptian forces under Ibrahim Pasha destroy Diriyah, ending the First Saudi State.
Turki ibn Abdullah revives Saudi rule by establishing the Second Saudi State with Riyadh as its capital.
The rival Rashidi dynasty defeats the Saudis at the Battle of Mulayda, ending the Second Saudi State and exiling the Al Saud.
Abdulaziz ibn Saud recaptures Riyadh in a daring night raid, launching the rise of the modern Saudi state.
Ibn Saud seizes the eastern al-Hasa region from the Ottomans, gaining the Persian Gulf coast.
Ibn Saud conquers the Hejaz, capturing Mecca, Medina, and Jeddah from the Hashemite Sharif Hussein.
Ibn Saud unifies his domains into the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, proclaiming himself its first king.
Commercial quantities of oil are discovered at Dammam, transforming the kingdom's economic future.
Saudi Arabia becomes a founding member of the United Nations and Ibn Saud meets U.S. President Roosevelt aboard the USS Quincy.
King Abdulaziz ibn Saud dies and is succeeded by his son Saud, while the Council of Ministers is formalized.
Saudi Arabia becomes a founding member of OPEC, shaping global oil policy.
King Saud is deposed in favor of his brother Faisal, who launches sweeping modernization reforms.
Saudi Arabia leads an OPEC oil embargo against Western backers of Israel, triggering a global energy crisis.
King Faisal is assassinated by a nephew and is succeeded by his brother Khalid.
Militants seize Mecca's Grand Mosque in a two-week siege, prompting a turn toward stricter religious conservatism.
King Fahd ascends the throne and later adopts the title Custodian of the Two Holy Mosques.
Saudi Arabia hosts U.S.-led coalition forces after Iraq's invasion of neighboring Kuwait.
The kingdom serves as the main base for the Gulf War that liberates Kuwait from Iraqi occupation.
King Fahd issues the Basic Law of Governance, codifying the country's system of rule.
A wave of al-Qaeda terrorist attacks strikes Riyadh, prompting a major domestic counterterrorism campaign.
King Abdullah succeeds Fahd and the country holds its first nationwide municipal elections.
King Salman accedes to the throne and Saudi Arabia begins a military intervention in Yemen's civil war.
Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman unveils Vision 2030, a plan to diversify the economy beyond oil.
A decades-old ban on women driving is lifted, and the killing of journalist Jamal Khashoggi draws global condemnation.
Saudi Aramco launches the world's largest initial public offering on the Tadawul stock exchange.
The kingdom announces a target of net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2060 as part of economic diversification efforts.
